Machine Learning in Materials Processing & Characterization
Unit 4: From Classical Metrics to Learned Representations

Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz

FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg

01. Where We Are

Recap — Unit 3

  • Cleaning, scaling, transforms, leakage-safe validation.
  • Every preprocessing choice is a modelling decision (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Today — Unit 4

  • Turn microstructure into vectors a model can use.
  • Classical scalars → hand-crafted vectors → \(S_2\) / MKS → (next week) CNNs on pixels.

Anchor. Unit 2 gave the measurement chain; Unit 3 gave honest tensors and splits. Today is the bridge to architectures (MFML) and to spatial models (Unit 5). Connect. Standardisation and group splits from Unit 3 reappear in §3 and §4 as non-negotiables.

02. Learning Outcomes

By the end of this unit, you can:

  1. Quantify information loss when a micrograph becomes one stereological scalar.
  2. Choose hand-crafted descriptors vs learned / correlation-based inputs for a task.
  3. Encode microstructure as tabular, \(S_2\), or image input (and know which architecture matches).
  1. Apply the MFML MLP toolkit to tabular materials prediction (McClarren 2021).
  2. Interpret what an MLP emphasises vs what a human would report (e.g. tail vs mean grain size).
  3. Name failure modes (split, shift, labels, imbalance, raw pixels) that motivate CNNs (Unit 5).

Anchor. Outcomes 4–6 assume MFML Unit 4 (forward pass, activations, backprop) — this deck does not re-derive them. Pitfall. If students skipped MFML, point them to the MFML deck before the case study in §4.

03. The Language of Microstructures

Traditional

  • Human-defined metrics: grain size, phase fraction, \(S_V\).
  • Standards-driven (ASTM, DIN) — reproducible, auditable.

Modern

  • Embeddings: algorithm-built vectors that retain task-relevant structure.
  • Goal: link processing → structure → property with less hand-picked loss (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Note

MFML this morning: MLP forward pass, activations, training loop. We recap in §2, then focus on materials encodings.

War story. Same micrograph, three different segmentations → three different “grain sizes.” The number is not objective; the pipeline is. Connect. §1 makes the lossy scalar explicit; §3 adds vectors that keep more morphology.

§1 · Classical Microstructure Metrics

04. Stereology: Quantifying Structure

Stereology — estimate 3D quantities from 2D sections.

  • \(V_V\) — volume fraction per phase.
  • \(S_V\) — interface area per unit volume.
  • Mean intercept — linked to grain size (standards).

Anchor in Unit 2

  • A section is one draw from a 3D microstructure; estimators have variance.
  • Reporting \(N\) sections and how boundaries were detected matters as much as the number.

Anchor. Tie stereology to the sensing chain: thresholding and edge detection are part of the “instrument.” Pitfall. Never compare ASTM numbers across labs without the segmentation protocol.

05. Stereological Descriptors in Practice

Scalars from rich images

  • \(\bar{d}\), \(\phi_\alpha\), \(V_V^{\text{pore}}\) — each is one summary.
  • Hand-crafted features: named, interpretable, lossy.
  • Segmentation choices feed directly into every scalar (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Anchor. Unit 3: choosing \(\bar{d}\) is as much a model as choosing an MLP — it encodes what you think matters. Pitfall. sem_grains.png is modest resolution; we keep display width moderate to avoid ugly upscaling on 1920×1080. Connect. §3 replaces “one number” with correlation vectors (\(S_2\)) and later with spatial nets.

06. Hand-Crafted Shape Descriptors

Families

  • Shape: aspect ratio, circularity, tortuosity.
  • Distribution: nearest-neighbour spacing, clustering indices.
  • Texture: ODF coefficients, pole figures.

Trade-off

  • Strength: physical names, peer-reviewable.
  • Weakness: you only recover structure you designed the scalar to see — unknown mechanisms stay invisible.

Materials example. Fatigue life often correlates with tail grain size and pore clusters — not with a single mean intercept. Connect. §4 shows an MLP finding tail-sensitive combinations on the same 12 scalars a linear model uses.

07. The Information Bottleneck

  • Micrograph: \(\mathcal{O}(10^6)\) pixels of state (before compression).
  • ASTM-style scalar: one number per channel of interest.
  • Order-of-magnitude: vast compression — most spatial detail discarded by construction.

Question: keep more information without \(10^6\)-dim raw pixels?

Answer: structured vectors — \(S_2\), descriptor stacks, learned embeddings — sized to data and task.

Pitfall. “More features” without more specimens or regularisation → overfit (see §4 failure modes). Connect. MKS and eigen-microstructures (§3) are two disciplined ways to grow \(D\) without jumping to full pixels yet.

08. PSPP as a Feature Space

Processing \(\to\) structure (metric \(d\)) \(\to\) property (e.g. hardness \(H\)).

Hall–Petch (illustrative):

\[H = H_0 + k\, d^{-1/2}\]

Linear in \(d^{-1/2}\) — a physics-first coordinate choice.

Role of ML: learn \(H = f(\mathbf{s})\) when the true \(\mathbf{s}\) is not one scalar (McClarren 2021).

Anchor. PSPP is the course backbone — today we only upgrade the “S” box from one number to a vector representation. Pitfall. Hall–Petch breaks outside its validity window; a linear fit is not a law of nature everywhere in process space.

09. Why Not Just Metrics?

Systems that break “one scalar per mechanism”

  • High-entropy alloys — multi-phase, partitioning, sluggish diffusion.
  • Nanocomposites — nm–µm hierarchy in one image.
  • Additive manufacturing — spatially varying solidification, not a single stationary field.

Consequence

  • Scalar summaries assume stationarity and known relevant descriptors.
  • When morphology is multi-scale or path-dependent, the “S” in PSPP needs a richer basis than ASTM charts alone (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

War story. Plot hardness vs mean \(d\) only — looks noisy. Add tail diameter and pore clustering features (or \(S_2\)) — structure appears. The noise was missing coordinates, not the lab. Connect. Same theme as weakest-link in §4.

10. The Paradigm Shift

Classical Modern
Input Image \(\to\) metrics Image / field \(\to\) representation
Features Hand-crafted Learned or correlation-based
Limit Information loss Data + compute + validation discipline

Shift in practice

  • From “predict with descriptors” to “learn (or engineer) a representation, then predict.”
  • Scientist still owns labels, splits, metrics, physics checks (Neuer et al. 2024).

Pitfall. “Let the network find features” does not remove responsibility for leakage, shift, or nonsense correlations (Unit 3 + §4). Connect. Unit 5 adds the right inductive bias for pixels (locality + weight sharing).

11. §1 Recap

  1. Stereology = standards-grade 3D inference from 2D — with estimator variance.
  2. Hand-crafted scalars = interpretable and biased by what you measured.
  3. Bottleneck: \(10^6\) pixels \(\to\) one number is extreme compression.
  1. PSPP + Hall–Petch = toy for linear structure–property links.
  2. Complex materials need richer “S” than a single chart metric.
  3. Next: MFML toolkit (§2) then encodings (§3).

Use as checkpoint. 60-second pair share: name one material where a single ASTM number is known to be misleading — and what extra coordinate you would add.

§2 · MFML Recap — The NN Toolkit

12. What MFML Covered This Morning

  • Neuron: \(a = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)\).
  • MLP: stack of layers; composes nonlinear features from \(\mathbf{x}\).
  • Universal approximation: wide enough shallow net + nonlinearity → dense in continuous functions (hypotheses on domain / regularity) (McClarren 2021).
  • Training: minimise loss \(J(\hat{y}, y)\) by gradient-based updates; backprop = efficient chain rule (Neuer et al. 2024).
  • No re-derivation here — MFML Unit 4 deck is the reference.

Anchor. This slide is signposting only; depth lives in MFML. Connect. §4 uses the same toolkit on tabular microstructure descriptors — fair comparison to linear baselines.

13. Activation Cheat-Sheet

Location Task Activation
Hidden Generic ReLU / Leaky ReLU
Output Regression Linear (\(\mathbb{R}\))
Output Binary Sigmoid \((0,1)\)
Output Multi-class Softmax (simplex)

Materials example

  • Phase ID: \(\bigl(P_{\mathrm{FCC}}, P_{\mathrm{BCC}}, P_{\mathrm{HCP}}\bigr)\) from softmax — not argmax until you understand calibration needs (Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville 2016).

Pitfall. Softmax “probabilities” are only well-calibrated under correct modelling assumptions — treat as scores first, then calibrate if needed (later units). Connect. Unit 12 revisits probabilistic outputs more seriously.

14. The Training Loop in One Slide

Invariant across domains

  • Same loop for steel design sheets and for \(S_2\) vectors.

What changes in materials ML

  • What is in \(X\) (encoding), what \(y\) means (measurement chain), how you split (specimens, not crops) — rest of this lecture (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Anchor. Loss choice ties to Unit 3 metrics lecture (MAE vs RMSE vs classification metrics) — same \(J\), different reporting emphasis. Pitfall. Fitting normalisers on all data before split → leakage (Unit 3); applies unchanged to tabular microstructure inputs.

15. Why This Toolkit on Materials Data

The MFML deck proves flexibility. Materials questions:

  1. What feeds \(\mathbf{x}\)? — pixels, descriptors, \(S_2\), process vector?
  2. Who labels \(y\)? — scarce expert time; label error budgets.
  3. Did we learn physics or nuisance? — probes, etching, detector gain.
  4. When does an MLP on raw micrographs fail? — parameter count vs \(N\) → CNNs (Unit 5).

Note

If any MFML step is fuzzy, pause here and reopen the MFML deck — §3–§4 assume the vocabulary above is fluent.

Connect. This list is the rubric for reading papers: always ask those four before trusting a leaderboard score.

§3 · Encoding Microstructures for ML

16. The Encoding Question

Before training: map microstructure to tensor \(\mathbf{X}\).

Principle: encoding upper-bounds what physics the hypothesis class can express — garbage encoding, garbage in (Neuer et al. 2024).

Encoding Shape Model sees
Hand-crafted \(\mathbb{R}^{D}\), small Pre-distilled features
Flattened image \(\mathbb{R}^{HW}\) Pixels, no locality prior
\(S_2\) / patches \(\mathbb{R}^{D'}\) Correlations / local stats
Image + conv \(\mathbb{R}^{H\times W\times C}\) Spatial features (Unit 5)

Pitfall. Using \(10^4\)-dim flattened pixels with \(N=200\) specimens → memorisation (§4, slide 38). Connect. Rows are ordered by increasing inductive bias + increasing engineering for spatial data.

17. Hand-Crafted Descriptors Revisited

Part 1 features — grain stats, ODF coefficients, porosity summaries — are one legitimate vectorisation \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^D\).

  • Every component has a name and (often) a unit.
  • Good for audits, baselines, and physics storytelling.

Limits

  • Biased toward what metrologists historically measured.
  • Information you never encoded cannot be invented by an MLP on that fixed \(\mathbf{x}\) — you need richer inputs or a different architecture.

War story. Same 12 numbers, linear vs MLP (§4): nonlinearity discovers interactions, not new measurements. New physics requires new columns or new representation.

18. Two-Point Statistics \(S_2\)

\[S_2(\mathbf{r}) = P\bigl(\text{phase}(\mathbf{x})=\alpha \;\wedge\; \text{phase}(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{r})=\alpha\bigr)\]

  • Translation-averaged microstructural correlation.
  • Captures length scales, anisotropy, clustering — more than one scalar, still compact vs pixels.

Why MLP-friendly

  • Fixed-length vector after binning \(\mathbf{r}\) on a grid in the unit cell / ROI.
  • Pairs well with standardised inputs (Unit 3) before stacking layers (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Anchor. \(S_2\) is the same “summary of spatial dependence” idea as covariance structure — but for phases / fields, not Gaussian assumptions only. Pitfall. Binning choice and ROI size change \(S_2\); document them like any other preprocessing step.

19. \(n\)-Point Statistics and Microstructure Functions

  • \(S_2\) is the lowest nontrivial correlation in the \(S_n\) family.
  • Higher \(S_n\) → more morphological detail, higher \(D\), heavier data requirements.
  • Many microstructure analyses stop at \(S_2\) + maybe a few higher terms deliberately (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Pragmatic rule

  • Prefer \(S_2\) + good labels + honest CV over raw pixels + tiny \(N\).
  • Escalate representation complexity only when CV and physics checks justify it.

Connect. Next slide: MKS operationalises \(S_2 \to\) property with a learned map — the standard “physics-informed compression” pattern in computational materials.

20. Materials Knowledge System (MKS) — Pipeline View

Typical MKS-style chain

  1. Segment / phase-label microstructure (or use phase-field output on a grid).
  2. Compute \(S_2\) (and optional higher correlations) on a fixed grid of \(\mathbf{r}\).
  3. Standardise correlation components using train statistics only (Unit 3).
  4. Train MLP (or linear map) \(g_\theta(S_2) \approx\) property or homogenised modulus.

Why it works

  • Bakes in translation invariance before the net sees data.
  • Keeps \(D\) in the hundreds, not millions — matches materials sample counts (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Pitfall. Treating simulation voxels and SEM pixels as interchangeable without resolution and noise models — domain shift in disguise (Unit 6 theme). Connect. Eigen-microstructures (next slide) are the PCA twin idea: orthogonal modes of field variation instead of explicit \(S_2\) bins.

21. Eigen-Microstructures — Preview

Idea: stack registered microstructure fields (phase indicator, orientation channel, etc.) into a big design matrix; PCA on standardised columns/voxels yields dominant modes of variation — “eigen-microstructures.”

Why standardise first? (Unit 3)

  • Without it, PC1 often tracks brightness / thickness / detector gain — not microstructure.
  • With per-feature z-scores fit on train only, PCs more often reflect shape variation (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Connect: Unit 5 CNNs learn spatial features end-to-end when \(N\) and inductive bias allow; eigen-modes are a linear compression baseline.

Pitfall. Alignment / cropping must be consistent across specimens; misregistration creates fake “modes.” Connect. This is the same “right coordinates before PCA” lesson as Unit 3 on tabular data — different object, same discipline.

22. Compositional and Process Descriptors

Often no image in \(X\) — tabular only:

  • Composition — mass or atomic fractions in \(\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{el}}}\).
  • Process — temperatures, times, cooling rate, atmosphere.
  • History — ordered steps (encoding choices: embeddings, discretised time bins, …).

MLP is natural here

  • \(D \sim 10\)–\(50\), well-defined units.
  • Standardise each column from training folds; same \((\mu,\sigma)\) at inference (Neuer et al. 2024).

Materials example. Steel strength from C–Mn–Cr–Ni–Mo + tempering recipe — classic MLP tabular regression benchmarked against thermodynamic/knowledge-based models. Pitfall. Composition features that sum to 1 are collinear — drop one element or use compositional geometry if you go beyond naive z-scores.

23. Tabular vs Structural Inputs

Input type Typical \(D\) First-line architecture
Composition + process 10–50 MLP
Morphology scalars 5–50 MLP
\(S_2\) / MKS vector \(10^2\)–\(10^3\) MLP / shallow 1D conv
Raw 2D micrograph \(10^4\)–\(10^7\) CNN (Unit 5)

Decision rule

  • Start with the smallest \(X\) that passes physics + CV tests.
  • Add representation capacity when grouped CV shows a persistent gap, not when train loss wants it (McClarren 2021).

War story. Teams that jump to CNNs on 200 JPEGs without specimen splits — pretty training curves, useless deployment (§4, slide 34).

24. The Information Bottleneck — Revisited

Pipeline Information retained (sketch)
1 scalar (ASTM \(G\)) Minimal
12 hand-crafted + MLP Low–mid
\(S_2\) + MLP Mid
CNN end-to-end High — if data allow

Trade-off

  • More coordinates \(\not\Rightarrow\) better test error without more independent specimens and careful validation.
  • This tension sets the agenda for Units 5–8 (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Anchor. Same “bias–variance” language as Unit 3 — richer \(X\) reduces bias but increases variance unless \(N\) grows or structure is regularised.

25. Standardisation Is Non-Negotiable

  • Mass fraction in \([0,1]\) vs temperature in \([300,1500]\) K → conditioned optimisation problem; gradients fight anisotropic curvature (Neuer et al. 2024).
  • Recipe: \(\tilde{x}_j = (x_j - \mu_j)/\sigma_j\) with \((\mu_j,\sigma_j)\) from train only; apply frozen values to val/test.

Link to leakage (Unit 3)

  • Recomputing \(\mu,\sigma\) on train+test before split leaks test marginals into \(X_{\mathrm{train}}\).
  • Put scaler + model in one pipeline object when using sklearn-style tooling (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Connect. Eigen-microstructures (slide 21) fail the same way if “standardise on all images” before CV.

26. Splitting Materials Data — The Specimen Rule

  • Do not IID-split crops or patches from the same coupon — they share processing, thickness, imaging session.
  • Do split by specimen / batch / furnace load — whatever is the exchangeable unit for deployment.

Why papers explode

  • Random patch split → optimistic \(R^2\); specimen split reveals truth (§4, slide 34) (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Forward: Unit 6 — transfer and domain shift formalise when train and test are not exchangeable.

War story. 16 crops × 200 specimens → 3200 rows feels big; effective \(N\) is still 200 physical draws from process variability. Pitfall. Time-ordered processing (heat-treatment line) also forbids naive shuffle — use temporal splits when causality matters (MFML + Unit 7 later).

27. §3 Recap

  1. Encoding = hard ceiling on learnable physics (for fixed architecture).
  2. Hand-crafted vectors = auditable; \(S_2\) / MKS = structured spatial stats without pixels.
  3. Eigen-microstructures = PCA on registered, standardised fields — linear bridge to CNN week.
  1. Tabular composition/process = default MLP turf.
  2. Standardise and split by specimen — same ethics as Unit 3, different column names.
  3. Next: case study — same 12 numbers, linear vs MLP (§4).

Checkpoint. One minute: students write down their own dataset’s natural group ID for GroupKFold-style splits.

§4 · Case Study — ASTM Grain Size vs. MLP

28. The Task

Predict tensile strength of a polycrystalline alloy from microstructure + measured strength labels.

  • \(N_{\mathrm{spec}}}=200\) — one calibrated SEM + one tensile coupon each.
  • Goal: generalise to new specimens, not new crops of old ones.

Experimental discipline

  • Same polishing / etching protocol; document magnification and segmentation version.
  • Strength measured with traceable gauge length / strain rate (Unit 2 chain).

Pitfall. If strength labels come from different test standards, you are mixing \(y\) distributions — structure signal dilutes.

29. Three Competing Models

Model Input # params (order)
Linear Hall–Petch \(d^{-1/2}\) only 2
Linear + descriptors 12 scalars 13
MLP Same 12 scalars \(\sim 10^3\)

Fair comparison

  • Identical \(X\) columns for the two supervised learners — only the composition rule \(f\) changes (McClarren 2021).

Anchor. This isolates nonlinearity vs new measurements — pedagogically clean. Pitfall. If the MLP had extra hidden inputs not given to linear models, the comparison is unfair — avoid.

30. Results (Illustrative)

Method Features \(R^2\) (test)
Hall–Petch Mean grain only 0.72
Linear + 12 Hand-crafted 0.81
MLP Same 12 0.88

Reading

  • +0.07 \(R^2\) vs linear on identical \(X\) → gains from feature interactions, not from sneaking in extra pixels.
  • Still need specimen-level CV and uncertainty — table alone is not a publication (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Pitfall. One lucky train/test split — always show CV spread or bootstrap CI on \(R^2\).

31. What Did the MLP Discover?

Permutation importance (storyline)

  • Largest grain features dominate → weakest-link fracture thinking, not mean-only Hall–Petch.
  • Pore-size dispersion beats total porosity → clustered damage matters.
  • Mean grain size mid-ranked → bulk strengthening present but not sufficient statistic.

Physical reading

  • Network re-weighted known scalars toward tail-sensitive combinations humans often under-report in tables.

Connect. Same moral as slide 06 — tail morphology matters; linear models in \(d^{-1/2}\) alone cannot express that without extra constructed features (which the MLP implicitly builds).

32. Where the Linear Model Fails

Linear in \(d^{-1/2}\) ties strength change to one aggregated size channel — cannot distinguish “halve mean \(d\)” vs “halve largest \(d\)” when processing paths differ in how they reshape the distribution.

MLP freedom

  • Can approximate \(\sigma_y \approx f(\bar{d}, d_{\max}, \mathrm{Var}[d], \ldots)\) without committing to a single global index upfront — still only as good as the 12 provided columns (McClarren 2021).

Pitfall. Students may think the MLP “invented” new microstructural information — it recombined existing scalars; new microphysics still needs new measurements or \(S_2\)/pixels.

33. When to Prefer the Linear Model

  • Very small \(N\) — MLP variance dominates; linear is a strong implicit regulariser.
  • Extrapolation outside training process window — linear may fail honestly vs wildly confident MLPs.
  • Regulatory / safety — sparse linear coefficients easier to audit than deep weights.

Practice

  • Always fit a serious linear baseline + grouped CV first.
  • Upgrade to MLP only if CV gain survives stress tests (shift, OOD batches) (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

War story. In licensing hearings, “black box beat linear by 2% on one split” loses to “linear model with documented coefficients and conservative bounds.”

34. Failure Mode — Train/Test Split Done Wrong

Invalid protocol

  1. 200 micrographs → 16 crops each → 3200 patches.
  2. Random 80/20 patch split.
  3. Report \(R^2 \approx 0.95\).

Reality

  • Train and test share specimens → correlated rows; metric is optimistic fiction.
  • Specimen-level split on same labels can collapse \(R^2\) toward 0.72 (illustrative) — the honest number (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Anchor. Direct application of Unit 3 group leakage — group = specimen ID. Pitfall. Even “stratified k-fold” on patches is wrong if stratification ignores specimen.

35. Failure Mode — Cross-Lab Distribution Shift

  • Train on microscope A: \(R^2 \approx 0.88\).
  • Test on microscope B (same alloy): \(R^2 \approx 0.45\).
  • Model may latch onto contrast / brightness / noise textures, not grains.

Mitigations (preview)

  • Physics-aware normalisation, harmonised imaging SOPs.
  • Domain randomisation / adaptation — Unit 6 when train and deployment labs differ (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Connect. Same failure mode hits CNNs harder because they see raw nuisance textures — another reason for careful augmentation + normalisation in Unit 5.

36. Failure Mode — Label Noise from Upstream Segmentation

  • \(y\) might be pristine tensile data while \(X\) descriptors come from a segmentation model or semi-automatic threshold.
  • Small systematic boundary errors → wrong grain/pore statistics → label noise in \(X\) viewed as aleatory scatter in \(y\).

Consequences

  • MLP fits segmentation artefacts correlated with instrument or operator session.
  • Mitigate: inter-annotator study on a subset, report label variance, ensemble segmentations, or uncertainty-aware losses (forward: Unit 12) (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Anchor. Unit 3 inter-annotator variance slide — same spirit, but here the “annotator” is a segmentation network version v1.3 vs v1.4. Pitfall. Retraining segmentation without versioning downstream strength models — silent pipeline break.

37. Failure Mode — Class Imbalance (Defect vs Nominal)

  • If defective specimens are rare (say 5%), a patch-level classifier can achieve high accuracy by always predicting “good.”
  • Materials goal is often high recall on the rare class — different metric (Unit 3).

Mitigations

  • Stratified splits by defect label at specimen level; report precision/recall/F1, not accuracy alone (Sandfeld et al. 2024).
  • Cost-sensitive losses, resampling within train only (watch leakage), or active labelling of hard negatives.

War story. “99% accuracy” on porosity detection when porosity appears in 2% of patches — model predicts “no pore” always; production disaster. Connect. Same metric hygiene as Unit 3 confusion-matrix lecture.

38. Failure Mode — Raw High-Resolution Micrographs

  • \(1024\times1024\) RGB → first dense layer has \(\mathcal{O}(10^6)\) input dims → \(\sim 10^9\) first-layer weights if fully connected.
  • With \(N \sim 10^2\) specimens, optimisation finds spurious pixel correlations (coupons, scratches, brightness gradients).

Take-away

  • CNNs (Unit 5): locality + weight sharing shrink effective parameters and respect 2D structure (Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville 2016).
  • Until then: compress to descriptors / \(S_2\) / eigen-modes with honest CV.

Connect. This slide is the single clearest motivation for the next lecture’s architecture family. Pitfall. Downscaling JPEG to make MLP tractable destroys fine porosity — physics loss, not a numerical trick.

39. §4 Recap

  1. Same 12 scalars: MLP > linear by exploiting interactions (weakest-link story).
  2. Linear still wins on tiny \(N\), extrapolation politics, audit needs.
  3. Specimen splits and lab shift dominate reported generalisation — not hidden-layer count.
  1. Segmentation noise and class imbalance can fake success metrics.
  2. Raw pixels + MLP = parameter–data mismatch → CNN week.

Use as exam prompt. “Explain why patch shuffling inflated \(R^2\); propose a correct split and two metrics beyond accuracy for rare defects.”

§5 · Wrap-Up

40. Putting It Together — A Materials Representation Recipe

  1. Define the deployment unit — specimen, batch, line, day.
  2. Choose \(X\) — start tabular / hand-crafted; escalate to \(S_2\) / eigen-modes / CNN only with evidence.
  3. Standardise using train statistics inside CV folds; pipeline everything.
  1. Split by group IDs that match deployment independence.
  2. Pick metrics that reflect cost of errors (recall on defects, not vanity accuracy).
  3. Sanity-check — linear baseline, permutation tests, stress on held-out lab / condition (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Closing mnemonic. Define unit → choose coordinates → standardise → split → metric → stress-test. Loop back when any step fails diagnostics.

41. Things Most Teams Get Wrong

  • Patch-level IID split with grouped data.
  • Scaler fit on all data before CV.
  • Accuracy on rare events; ignoring precision/recall.
  • Segmentation v1 labels treated as ground truth forever.
  • Tuning on test set (same as Unit 3 “optimistic final number”).
  • Declaring victory on one split without variance bars.
  • Skipping cross-instrument or cross-site held-out evaluation when deployment will move (Neuer et al. 2024).

Use as checklist. Ask students which items their last project violated — usually more than one.

42. Looking Ahead — Unit 5 (CNNs)

Next week

  • Convolutional nets for local patterns with shared filters across space.
  • Applications: phase segmentation, defect detection, micrograph classification — when \(N\) and imaging SOPs can support pixels.

Carry forward from today

  • Specimen splits, normalisation, shift awareness — CNNs do not remove these obligations; they multiply debugging surface (Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville 2016).

Teaser. Show one CNN saliency failure in lecture if time — network attends to scale bar or vignetting; connects back to slides 35–38.

43. Reading and Exercises

Reading

  • Sandfeld, Materials Data Science — Ch. 17 (nets), microstructure / stats chapters you use for \(S_2\) and CV (Sandfeld et al. 2024).
  • McClarren — Ch. 5 (feed-forward nets, capacity) (McClarren 2021).
  • Neuer — Ch. 4 (supervised learning workflow, losses) (Neuer et al. 2024).

Suggested exercises

  1. Reproduce linear vs MLP on a public tabular materials set with GroupKFold by alloy lot.
  2. Compute a binned \(S_2\) vector for one binary microstructure image set; compare MLP CV to 12 hand-crafted scalars.
  3. Deliberately leak scaler fit on full data; quantify CV inflation vs clean pipeline (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

Pacing. Assign exercise 3 as a 20-line sklearn notebook — high pedagogical ROI vs lecture time.

44. Key Takeaways

  • Classical metrics — interpretable, standardised, lossy; know what your scalar threw away.
  • Hand-crafted + MLP — strongest first upgrade when \(X\) is tabular and \(N\) is modest.
  • \(S_2\) / MKS / eigen-modes — principled middle ground before pixel CNNs (Sandfeld et al. 2024).
  • Standardise + split by specimen — same professional ethics as Unit 3; different filenames.
  • Shift, segmentation noise, imbalance — can erase real gains from better architectures (Neuer et al. 2024).
  • MLP on raw huge images — wrong tool; CNNs next week when the physics lives at pixel scale (Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville 2016).

End rhythm. Six fragments — pause on the last: CNNs are not magic; they are the right inductive bias when data, splits, and metrics are honest.

References

Goodfellow, Ian, Yoshua Bengio, and Aaron Courville. 2016. Deep Learning. MIT Press.
McClarren, Ryan G. 2021. Machine Learning for Engineers: Using Data to Solve Problems for Physical Systems. Springer.
Neuer, Michael et al. 2024. Machine Learning for Engineers: Introduction to Physics-Informed, Explainable Learning Methods for AI in Engineering Applications. Springer Nature.
Sandfeld, Stefan et al. 2024. Materials Data Science. Springer.

© Philipp Pelz - Machine Learning in Materials Processing & Characterization
